content.text: Tests fail as expected — `CsvReportLib.cs` doesn't exist yet. That's our RED state. Now let me write the implementation (GREEN phase).

content.thinking:
Now I need to think about the approach carefully. The Roslyn scripting approach is complex and fragile. Let me reconsider.

Actually, the problem with using Roslyn scripting to test is that it's slow and fragile. A simpler approach for .NET 10 file-based apps would be:

1. Write the library code in `CsvReportLib.cs` as a regular C# file with classes
2. Write tests that directly reference the library types

But wait — .NET 10 file-based apps can't reference other .cs files directly. Each `.cs` file runs independently. So I have a few options:


Option A uses Roslyn scripting to dynamically load the library, Option B embeds the library code in the test file via `#load` or source embedding, and Option C creates a proper xunit test project alongside the script. I'm not sure if `#load` even works in .NET 10 file-based apps though. The most straightforward path is to keep the library as a standalone file and have the test file compile it dynamically via Roslyn, then write the main app separately.

For the app itself, I'm realizing that in .NET 10 file-based apps each file is isolated, so `app.cs` would either need to duplicate the library code or compile it the same way the tests do. The cleanest structure would be to keep the library in its own file and have both the tests and app read and compile it, or just include all the code directly in `app.cs` for simplicity. I'm realizing the core issue: Roslyn scripting creates types that don't match the calling code's types, making dynamic casting unreliable. The cleanest solution is to have a single `CsvReportLib.cs` file with just the class definitions, then embed that source directly in `tests.cs` so everything compiles together in one unit, while `app.cs` does the same. I'm realizing I can actually use xunit as a proper test project since the requirements allow it, so I don't need to duplicate code or rely on unsupported features like #load directives. Let me go with a standard xunit setup instead of trying to force everything into file-based apps. I'm settling on the simplest approach: duplicate the library classes in both `app.cs` and `tests.cs` files, with each file containing its own top-level statements and class definitions. It's not ideal, but it's the cleanest way to work within .NET 10's single-file constraints without needing a project file. I'm going to take the practical route and make each file self-contained—tests.cs will have all the classes plus test logic, app.cs will have the same classes plus the main application, and I'll accept the code duplication as a trade-off of working without a project system.

content.text:
The Roslyn scripting approach adds unnecessary complexity. Let me take a simpler, more robust approach: self-contained files where library code is shared by including it directly. This is the practical trade-off of .NET 10 file-based apps.

Let me rewrite with a cleaner architecture:

RED phase confirmed — tests fail because the library doesn't exist. Now let me think about the approach. Using Roslyn scripting to cross-compile is complex and fragile. A simpler, more reliable approach for .NET 10 file-based apps is to put all code (library + tests) in self-contained files, and have the main app also be standalone. Let me restructure.

lines: // TDD Test Runner for CSV Report Generator
-// Uses a simple assertion framework — no external dependencies needed.
-// Each test follows red/green/refactor: tests were written BEFORE the implementation.
+// Approach: All library code is defined in this file (shared classes), with a
+// comprehensive test suite that exercises each component. The main app.cs will
+// duplicate the library types (or we include them inline). For .NET 10 file-based
+// apps, each .cs file is self-contained — no project references.
+// TDD cycle: tests were written FIRST (red), then implementation code was added
+// below to make them pass (green), then refactored.
+void AssertApprox(decimal expected, decimal actual, string message, decimal tolerance = 0.01m)
-// ─── Load the library under test via Roslyn scripting ───────────────────────────
-// We compile CsvReportLib.cs into a scripting session so we can call its types.
+// LIBRARY CODE UNDER TEST (same types used by app.cs)
+// --- CSV Parser: converts CSV text or files into Employee records ---
+    /// <summary>Parse CSV content from a string. First row must be the header.</summary>
+        if (lines.Length < 2) return employees; // header only or empty
-// Helper: continue the scripting session to call library methods
+                throw new FormatException($"Row {i} has {parts.Length} columns, expected 5: {line}");
+            if (!decimal.TryParse(parts[2].Trim(), NumberStyles.Any, CultureInfo.InvariantCulture, out var salary))
+            if (!DateTime.TryParse(parts[3].Trim(), CultureInfo.InvariantCulture, DateTimeStyles.None, out var hireDate))
+            throw new FileNotFoundException($"CSV file not found: {filePath}", filePath);
+// --- Aggregator: computes department and overall stats ---
+            throw new InvalidOperationException("Cannot compute statistics on an empty collection.");
+// --- Report Formatter: builds a human-readable text report ---
+    public static void Run(string inputCsvPath, string outputReportPath)
+            throw new InvalidOperationException("No active employees found in the data.");
Console.WriteLine("\n═══ CSV Report Generator — Test Suite ═══\n");
-// Test: Parse a simple CSV string into Employee records
+// Test: Parse a single CSV row
+    AssertEqual("Alice", employees[0].Name, "Parse single row: name = Alice");
+    AssertEqual("Engineering", employees[0].Department, "Parse single row: department = Engineering");
+    AssertEqual(new DateTime(2020, 1, 1), employees[0].HireDate, "Parse single row: hire_date");
+    AssertEqual("active", employees[0].Status, "Parse single row: status = active");
+    AssertEqual("A", employees[0].Name, "Parse multiple rows: first = A");
+    AssertEqual("B", employees[1].Name, "Parse multiple rows: second = B");
+// Test: Header-only CSV returns empty list
-    AssertEqual(0, list.Count, "Parse empty CSV (header only): count = 0");
+// Test: Empty string returns empty list
-        AssertTrue(true, "Parse invalid salary: throws on bad data");
+// Test: Too few columns throws FormatException
+    AssertTrue(threw, "Parse too few columns: throws FormatException");
-// Test: Filter to active employees only
+// Test: Filter keeps only active employees
-// Test: Filter when all are inactive
-    AssertEqual(0, list.Count, "ActiveOnly: returns 0 when all inactive");
+    AssertEqual(0, active.Count, "ActiveOnly: returns 0 when all inactive");
+    AssertEqual(2, active.Count, "ActiveOnly: keeps all when all active");
+// Test: OverallStats on empty list throws
+    var single = new List<Employee> { new("Solo", "IT", 75000m, DateTime.Now, "active") };
+    // Check that salary formatting is present (N2 format uses commas)
+    AssertTrue(report.Contains("90,000.00") || report.Contains("90000"), "Report: contains avg salary for Eng");
-// Test: Read from actual CSV file
-    AssertEqual(10, list.Count, "ParseFile: reads 10 rows from test_data.csv");
+    AssertEqual(10, employees.Count, "ParseFile: reads 10 rows from test_data.csv");
-// Test: Full pipeline — read CSV, filter, aggregate, write report
+// Test: ParseFile throws on missing file

+// Test: Full pipeline — CSV → filter → aggregate → report file

-        AssertTrue(true, "ParseFile missing file: throws descriptive error");

content.thinking:
The issue is that in .NET 10 file-based apps (top-level statements), type declarations like `record` and `class` need to come after the top-level statements, or we need to structure things differently. Actually, in C# 12+, top-level statements must precede namespace and type declarations. So I need to move all the class/record definitions to the bottom of the file (after the top-level statements), or use a different approach.

Let me restructure so that the type definitions are at the bottom of the file (after all top-level statements).

content.text: Need to move type declarations after top-level statements in C#. Let me restructure.

content.text:
**All 51 tests pass.** Here's a summary of the solution:

### Files created

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `test_data.csv` | Sample CSV fixture with 10 employees (8 active, 2 inactive) across 3 departments |
| `tests.cs` | Self-contained test runner with 51 assertions across 5 test categories + library code |
| `app.cs` | Standalone CLI app — `dotnet run app.cs -- input.csv [output.txt]` |
| `report.txt` | Generated sample report output |

### Architecture (5 components, all tested)

- **`CsvParser`** — Parses CSV strings/files into `Employee` records with validation
- **`EmployeeFilter`** — Filters to active employees (case-insensitive)
- **`Aggregator`** — Computes avg salary by dept, headcount by dept, and overall stats (min/max/avg/total/count)
- **`ReportFormatter`** — Builds a formatted text report with department and overall sections
- **`ReportPipeline`** — Orchestrates the full read → filter → aggregate → write flow

### Test coverage (51 assertions)

- **CSV Parsing (14)**: single/multi row, empty inputs, invalid salary/date/column count
- **Filtering (7)**: active-only, all-inactive, case-insensitive, all-active
- **Aggregation (12)**: avg salary, headcount, overall stats, empty-list error, single-employee edge case
- **Report Formatting (7)**: section headers, department names, salary figures
- **File I/O (11)**: file reading, missing file error, full pipeline integration, no-active-employees error

result:
**All 51 tests pass.** Here's a summary of the solution:

### Files created

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `test_data.csv` | Sample CSV fixture with 10 employees (8 active, 2 inactive) across 3 departments |
| `tests.cs` | Self-contained test runner with 51 assertions across 5 test categories + library code |
| `app.cs` | Standalone CLI app — `dotnet run app.cs -- input.csv [output.txt]` |
| `report.txt` | Generated sample report output |

### Architecture (5 components, all tested)

- **`CsvParser`** — Parses CSV strings/files into `Employee` records with validation
- **`EmployeeFilter`** — Filters to active employees (case-insensitive)
- **`Aggregator`** — Computes avg salary by dept, headcount by dept, and overall stats (min/max/avg/total/count)
- **`ReportFormatter`** — Builds a formatted text report with department and overall sections
- **`ReportPipeline`** — Orchestrates the full read → filter → aggregate → write flow

### Test coverage (51 assertions)

- **CSV Parsing (14)**: single/multi row, empty inputs, invalid salary/date/column count
- **Filtering (7)**: active-only, all-inactive, case-insensitive, all-active
- **Aggregation (12)**: avg salary, headcount, overall stats, empty-list error, single-employee edge case
- **Report Formatting (7)**: section headers, department names, salary figures
- **File I/O (11)**: file reading, missing file error, full pipeline integration, no-active-employees error
